name: azure-ai-contentsafety-ts
description: Analyze text and images for harmful content using Azure AI Content Safety (@azure-rest/ai-content-safety). Use when moderating user-generated content, detecting hate speech, violence, sexual content, or self-harm, or managing custom blocklists.
capabilities: Analyze text for hate speech, sexual content, violence, and self-harm indicators
Analyze images from base64 content or Azure blob URLs for harmful visual content
Create and manage custom blocklists for domain-specific prohibited terms
Return severity levels (0-6 or 0-7) for each detected category
Combine AI detection with custom blocklist matching for comprehensive moderation
Handle both API key and Azure AD (DefaultAzureCredential) authentication
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer produced 70 false positive findings by misidentifying markdown documentation as executable code. The SKILL.md file contains TypeScript code examples using markdown code fences (```), not shell backticks. The skill legitimately requires network access to Azure Content Safety APIs and environment variables for API credentials. No malicious patterns detected after manual review.
valueStatement: Protect your platform from harmful user-generated content including hate speech, violence, sexual content, and self-harm material. This skill integrates Azure AI Content Safety REST API to automatically analyze and flag inappropriate text and images with configurable severity thresholds.
userTitle: Moderate harmful content with Azure AI Content Safety
limitations: Requires Azure Content Safety resource and valid API credentials
Network connectivity to Azure Cognitive Services endpoint required
Image analysis limited to supported formats (PNG, JPEG, etc.)
Cannot analyze video content or real-time streaming media

useCases.targetUser: Community managers and social platform developers
useCases.title: Social Platform Content Moderation
useCases.description: Automatically scan user posts, comments, and uploaded images before publication to detect and block harmful content that violates community guidelines.

useCases.title: Educational Forum Safety
useCases.description: Protect students in online learning environments by filtering hate speech, bullying content, and self-harm discussions with appropriate severity thresholds.

useCases.title: E-commerce Review Filtering
useCases.description: Moderate product reviews and seller communications to maintain platform quality standards and prevent abusive or inappropriate content from appearing.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Text Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this text for harmful content and tell me if it should be allowed: "[INSERT TEXT]"
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick moderation of a single piece of text content

promptTemplates.title: Image Content Check
promptTemplates.prompt: Check if this image contains harmful or inappropriate content that should be blocked from our platform: [PROVIDE IMAGE PATH OR URL]

promptTemplates.title: Custom Blocklist Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create a blocklist for my gaming platform that blocks cheating-related terms, hate speech, and harassment. Add these specific terms: [LIST TERMS]
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up domain-specific content filtering rules

promptTemplates.title: Batch Content Moderation
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to moderate these 50 user submissions with a maximum allowed severity of 2 (low). Flag anything above this threshold and check against my "gaming-community" blocklist: [LIST CONTENT]
promptTemplates.scenario: High-volume content moderation with custom thresholds and blocklists

bestPractices: Always use the isUnexpected() type guard to handle API errors gracefully and prevent crashes from unexpected responses
Set category-specific severity thresholds based on your community guidelines - hate speech may need stricter limits than general violence
Maintain audit logs of all moderation decisions with timestamps, severity scores, and action taken for compliance and appeals
antiPatterns: Do not block content solely based on severity 2 (Low) - this may result in excessive false positives and user frustration
Never store API keys directly in code - always use environment variables or Azure Key Vault for credential management
Avoid making moderation decisions on empty text or corrupted images - validate input before sending to the API

faq.question: What categories of harmful content does Azure Content Safety detect?
faq.answer: Azure Content Safety detects four main categories: Hate (discriminatory language targeting identity groups), Sexual (sexual content, nudity, pornography), Violence (physical harm, weapons, terrorism), and SelfHarm (self-injury, suicide, eating disorders).

faq.question: How do severity levels work and what should I block?
faq.answer: Severity levels range from 0 (Safe) to 6 or 7 (High). Recommended actions: 0 = Allow, 2 = Review or allow with warning, 4 = Block or require human review, 6 = Block immediately. Configure thresholds based on your risk tolerance.

faq.question: Can I add custom words or phrases to block?
faq.answer: Yes. Use blocklists to define custom prohibited terms specific to your domain. Create a blocklist with the PATCH endpoint, add items with the addOrUpdateBlocklistItems action, then reference blocklistNames during text analysis.

faq.question: What image formats are supported for analysis?
faq.answer: Azure Content Safety supports common image formats including PNG, JPEG, BMP, and GIF. Images can be provided as base64-encoded content or as Azure Blob Storage URLs.

faq.question: How do I authenticate with the Azure Content Safety API?
faq.answer: Two options: (1) API Key authentication using AzureKeyCredential with your CONTENT_SAFETY_KEY, or (2) Azure AD authentication using DefaultAzureCredential for managed identity or service principal scenarios.

faq.question: What happens if the API is unavailable or returns an error?
faq.answer: Always wrap API calls with isUnexpected() type guard to detect errors. On failure, decide whether to block content defensively (fail-closed) or allow it temporarily (fail-open) based on your risk tolerance. Log all errors for investigation.
